title: Contacting Josephson Junctions via Airbridges in Superconducting Circuits
authors: Prakiran Baidya, Momčilo Milosavljević, Murali Krishna Kurmapu, Thomas Fösel, Harshanth Ram Murugesan, Victor Kemme, Mojahed Jaber, Markus Sondermann, Christopher Eichler
abstract: Superconducting circuit devices require electrical interconnects between different circuit elements on the chip, for which conventional device architectures use a combination of two structural elements: \textit{airbridges} to connect non-adjacent elements in the base layer, and \textit{bandages} to connect the electrodes forming the Josephson junctions to the base layer. Bandages introduce unwanted parasitic material interfaces and increase the manufacturing complexity. Here, we overcome the limitations imposed by \emph{bandages} by establishing \textit{all} electrical interconnects with airbridges of varying size fabricated in a single step. The airbridges show a high yield and mechanical stability over a wide range of sizes from $0.5\,μ\mathrm{m}$ to $4\,μ\mathrm{m}$ in width and from $5\,μ\mathrm{m}$ to $40\,μ\mathrm{m}$ in length, and show low loss when integrated in coplanar waveguide resonators and transmon qubits. Measured relaxation times up to more than $250\,μ\mathrm{s}$ in standard transmon geometries show that the process achieves high coherence while substantially easing and accelerating device fabrication.
summary: This paper introduces a novel, streamlined fabrication technique for connecting superconducting circuit elements using only airbridges. By eliminating complex 'bandage' contacts, the method maintains high device coherence and low loss while drastically simplifying manufacturing steps for transmon qubits.
main_problem: The primary challenge is developing reliable, low-loss electrical interconnects for superconducting quantum circuits that avoid the complexity and damage associated with conventional 'bandage' contacts.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrated a single-step airbridge fabrication process that maintains high coherence in transmon qubits (up to 290 $\mu$s) while significantly simplifying device manufacturing.
method: They fabricated interconnects using varying-sized airbridges via grayscale electron-beam lithography and characterized performance by measuring qubit relaxation times ($T_1$) and resonator quality factors ($Q$).
model_or_system: The system involves superconducting circuits, specifically transmon qubits coupled to Coplanar Waveguide (CPW) resonators. The interconnects are the airbridges themselves, which act as tunable elements affecting circuit parameters.
key_observables: Energy relaxation time ($T_1$), internal quality factor ($Q_i$), frequency shift ($\Delta f$), and room-temperature conductance ($G$).
important_parameters: Airbridge dimensions (widths $0.5 \mu	ext{m}$ to $4 \mu	ext{m}$, lengths $5 \mu	ext{m}$ to $40 \mu	ext{m}$), and the comparison between intrinsic loss and airbridge-induced loss.
main_assumptions: The process assumes that using only airbridges eliminates parasitic interfaces, though solvent choice (e.g., acetone vs NMP) is critical for successful lift-off.
figures_summary: Figures show SEM images of fabricated airbridges across various dimensions and structural profiles (linear vs parabolic). Other figures detail $T_1$ decay curves, resonator $S_{21}$ spectra showing frequency shifts, and comparisons of quality factors based on the number of airbridges.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from identifying the fabrication bottleneck (bandages) to proposing a single-airbridge solution. It then characterizes this structure electrically by measuring qubit coherence ($T_1$) and resonator response ($Q$, $\Delta f$), concluding with process optimization details.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses hardware limitations in superconducting quantum processors, which is central to realizing scalable quantum computation architectures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of a conventional two-element contacting scheme. Bandages (red) connect Josephson junctions (gray) to the base layer (light gray), whereas airbridges (blue) connect non-adjacent components of the base layer. (b) Schematic diagram showing the material stack during the in-situ ion milling step applied prior to depositing the bandage metal. (c) Schematic of a the single-element contacting scheme presented in this work, in which all connections are established via airbridges of varying size. (d) Same as (b) but for the case of airbridge connections. The bridge scaffold protects the silicon from exposure to ions.

FIG. 2. Gallery of scanning electron micrograph (SEM) images of (a) separate parts of the ground plane contacted via airbridges of length 36 µm and width 4 µm, (b) two segments of a coplanar waveguide crossing another one via airbridge of same dimension as in (a), and (c) airbridges having length 15 µm and width 3 µm contact Josephson junction electrodes to the base-layer metal that are ∼10 µm apart in a test structure. (d)-(e) Zoom into the contact areas between (d) airbridge and base layer and (e) airbridge and Josephson junction electrodes.

FIG. 3. (a) Optical image of one of the three resonator devices each of which contains 12 CPW resonators of varying length and cross-sectional dimensions as shown in the inset. The numerical label (blue) adjacent to each resonator indicates its index within the chip. (b) SEM image of the second device in which the center conductor is segmented via connecting airbridges ("CPW airbridges"). (c) Third device with airbridges across the CPW to connect the two halves of the ground plane ("Ground airbridges"). (d) Measured internal quality factors at approximately single photon level (Qi,LP) for the three devices, with individual data points, median (horizontal bar), and interquartile range (box)....

FIG. 4. (a) Optical image of a two-pad transmon device with a design similar to the one used in Ref. [13]. (b)-(c) SEM image of the section marked in (a) showing the airbridges connecting two junction electrodes to the base layer in (b), and an airbridge crossing a CPW in (c). (d) Measured qubit population (green circle) as a function of delay time for one of the transmon devices after an intial π-pulse. The dashed line is a fit to the data. (e) Repeated T1 measurements performed over 24 hours for the best performing qubit. (f) Measured T1 for four different qubits across three chips. The color saturation of data points indicates the qubit frequency f01 (see color bar).

FIG. 5. GDS layout of a typical airbridge design used for contacting – (a) junction to the base-layer, and (b) two non- adjacent ground planes across a CPW . For both panels (a) and (b), the shaded regions designate different parts of the GDS design as indicated. (c) Dose distribution used to define the gradient exposure during gray-scale e-beam lithography process.
